1: Psalm 73 opens Book III of the Psalter (Ps 73-89) and is attributed to Asaph, the Levitical musician appointed by David (1 Chr 6:39; 15:17) who became the head of one of the three guilds of temple singers. Psalm 73 is one of the great wisdom-theodicy psalms of the OT, comparable to Job in its engagement with the problem of the wicked prospering. 'Truly God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart' — the psalm opens with the conclusion reached only after the crisis of vv. 2-16 and the resolution of v. 17. The opening statement is the post-sanctuary wisdom, not the naive assumption the psalmist held throughout. The psalm is structured as a retrospective: this is what I know now, having been through the crisis.
2: 'But as for me, my feet nearly slipped; my steps almost gave way' — the psalmist's near-apostasy: the 'slipping' and 'almost giving way' are the metaphors of losing footing on the covenant path. The 'slippery ground' (v. 18) on which the wicked stand becomes the ground on which the psalmist himself nearly slipped — the crisis was interior, not external. The psalm admits that the observation of the wicked's prosperity nearly destroyed the psalmist's own covenant faith.
3: 'For I was envious of the arrogant when I saw how the wicked prosper' — the theodicy problem stated plainly: seeing the wicked's visible, undeniable prosperity produces envy and the corresponding question about covenant justice. 'Arrogant' (holalim, boasters/fools) are those who live without reference to YHWH — the 'fool' (naval) of Ps 14:1 who says there is no God. The problem is not abstract but visceral: the psalmist saw the wicked prospering and found in this a specific challenge to the covenant premise that righteousness is rewarded.
4: 'For they have no anguish in their death; their bodies are sleek and strong' — the specific evidence: the wicked die without suffering. 'Sleek and strong' (bari ulam, fat and healthy) — the well-fed, physically robust condition of the prosperous wicked is the visible sign of their ease. In the ancient world, physical robustness was a sign of divine favor; the wicked's gleaming health contradicts the expected covenant correlation between wickedness and physical decline.
5: 'They are not in trouble like other people; they are not plagued like the rest of mankind' — the wicked's exemption from common human suffering: they do not share in the general troubles or in the plagues of humanity. This exemption from suffering is what makes their prosperity so theologically troubling: they lack not only punishment but even the normal burdens of human existence. They seem to operate under different rules.
6: 'Therefore pride adorns them like a necklace; violence wraps them like a garment' — the visual metaphor of pride and violence as clothing: in the ancient world, jewelry and fine garments were signs of wealth and status. The wicked wear pride as their ornament and violence as their robe — their social presentation is made of the very sins that should mark them for judgment. Instead of covenant virtues adorning them, their wickedness has become their badge of honor.
7: 'Their eyes bulge with prosperity; the desires of their heart exceed all bounds' — 'their eyes bulge from fat' is a satirical hyperbole of excessive wealth. In the ancient Near East, visible prosperity (full body, bright eyes, healthy skin) was a recognized sign of well-being. 'The desires of their heart exceed all bounds' — the imagination of the heart overflows; their desires cannot be contained by any moral or social limit.
8: 'They scoff and speak with malice; they talk of oppression with arrogance' — the social dimension of the wicked's prosperity: their speech is scoffing, malicious, and openly oppressive. The combination of prosperity and social dominance: they can say openly what others must whisper, and suffer no consequences. Their impunity in speech reflects their impunity in action.
9: 'They direct their mouths against the heavens, and their tongue ranges across the earth' — the spatial claim of the wicked's speech: from heaven to earth, their tongue sweeps across the entire created order. 'Mouths against the heavens' — speech directed upward as a challenge to divine authority (cf. Rev 13:6: the beast 'opened its mouth to blaspheme God'). The wicked's speech claims universal authority by challenging both the divine sphere (above) and the human sphere (below).
10: 'Therefore people keep returning to them, and a full cup of water is drained by them' — the social magnetism of power: people are attracted to the wicked precisely because of their prosperity and social dominance. The surrounding people empty themselves in service to the powerful. This is the patron-client dynamic at its most exploitative: the powerful attract clients who drain themselves in service, while the powerful accumulate further wealth and status.
11: 'They say, How can God know? Does the Most High have knowledge?' — the practical atheism of the prospering wicked: not denial of divine existence but denial of divine moral attention. If YHWH does not see or does not care about their conduct, the covenant framework of accountability is null. This is the same challenge as Ps 10:11-13 and Job 22:12-14 (Eliphaz's accusation that Job assumed God could not see through the clouds). The question is theological: does the covenant God actually monitor human conduct and respond to it?
12: 'See — these are the wicked, always at ease, growing in wealth' — the summary of the evidence: the wicked are 'at ease' (shalvei olam, at peace always) and 'growing in wealth.' The psalmist has made an empirical observation that challenges the Deuteronomic theology of covenant consequences. The observation is accurate; the theological problem it raises is genuine. The psalmist does not deny the evidence — he is wrestling with it honestly.
13: 'Surely I have kept my heart clean for nothing, and washed my hands in innocence in vain' — the crisis of covenant meaning: if the wicked prosper without consequence, covenant faithfulness (keeping the heart clean) and ritual purity (washing the hands — the declaration of innocence, as in Ps 26:6; Deut 21:6-7) are pointless. 'For nothing' (riq, in vain/empty) is the wisdom vocabulary of futility (Eccl 1:2: havel havalim, vanity of vanities). The psalmist is applying the vanity verdict specifically to the covenant life of righteousness.
14: 'For all day long I have been afflicted, and punishment comes every morning' — the personal experience that contrasts with the wicked's ease (v. 12: 'always at ease'): the psalmist is 'afflicted' (nagua, stricken — the same word used for leprosy in Lev 13 and for the Suffering Servant in Isa 53:4) 'all day long.' 'Punishment every morning' — while the wicked sleep soundly, each morning brings the psalmist fresh chastisement. The asymmetry is the pastoral and theological crisis.
15: 'If I had said, I will speak this way, I would have betrayed the generation of your children' — the check on apostasy: the psalmist did not speak the crisis aloud because doing so would have 'betrayed' the covenant community. The social responsibility of covenant speech: speaking the crisis theology (righteousness is pointless) publicly would have undermined the faith of those whose covenant trust depended on the community's collective testimony. The psalmist's internal struggle remained private precisely out of covenant loyalty to the community.
16: 'When I tried to make sense of all this, it was a torment before my eyes' — 'I tried to make sense of it' — the rational-theological effort to resolve the theodicy problem through reflection alone. It failed: the attempt produced 'torment' (amal, hard labor/misery) rather than resolution. The theodicy problem is not solvable by intellectual effort alone — the psalmist's wisdom categories cannot absorb the evidence he has observed. This is the limit of human wisdom confronted in Job and here.
17: 'Until I entered the sanctuary of God; then I grasped their end' — the resolution comes in the sanctuary (miqdeshei-el, the holy places of God). Not through additional reasoning but through the experience of the divine presence in worship. The sanctuary context shifts the psalmist's perspective: from the immanent time-frame (present prosperity of the wicked) to the transcendent time-frame (their final destiny). 'I grasped their end' — eschatological perspective on the wicked is only possible from within the space where divine reality is encountered: the temple, the sanctuary, the place of covenant worship.
18: 'Surely you have placed them on slippery ground; you cast them down to desolation' — the divine perspective on the wicked's position: what looks like solid prosperity is actually 'slippery ground' (chalaqot, smooth/slick places). The same word as 'nearly slipped' in v. 2: the psalmist who nearly slipped is actually on firmer ground than the wicked who seem secure. The wicked's ease is not a stable platform but a frictionless surface from which they will suddenly fall.
19: 'How suddenly they are destroyed, completely swept away by terrors!' — 'suddenly' — the speed of divine judgment on the wicked is the shocking reversal of their apparent permanence. 'Completely swept away by terrors' — the terrors that end the wicked are the same 'terrors' of death and judgment that characterized Sheol in ancient Israelite thought (Job 18:11, 14; 24:17: the 'terrors of death').
20: 'Like a dream that vanishes when one awakes, so when you arise, O Lord, you despise their fleeting form' — the dream image of the wicked's existence: vivid while it lasts, completely dissolved when morning comes. YHWH's 'arising' is the language of divine judicial intervention after apparent inaction (cf. Ps 44:23: 'Wake up! Why are you sleeping, O Lord?'). When YHWH acts in judgment, the wicked's substantial-seeming prosperity turns out to have been a dream-image with no lasting substance.
21: 'When my heart was embittered and I was pierced in my inner being' — the confession of the emotional and physical experience of the theodicy crisis. 'Embittered' (the verb used for sourdough leavening in Exod 12:19) and 'pierced in my inner being' (my kidneys were sharpened/pierced) — the kidneys/inner being was understood as the seat of deep emotion and conscience (cf. Job 16:13; Ps 16:7; 139:13). The crisis had penetrated to the deepest interior of the psalmist's emotional and moral life.
22: 'I was brutish and ignorant; I was no better than an animal before you' — the psalmist's self-assessment of his condition during the crisis: 'brutish (ba'ar, stupid/animal-like) and ignorant.' The animal comparison (behemot hayiti imach, I was like an animal before you) is the sharpest self-condemnation: the person who reduced the covenant relationship to earthly accounting had descended to the level of creatures who have no covenant knowledge. The sanctuary experience (v. 17) revealed not just the wicked's end but also the psalmist's own blindness.
23: 'Yet I am always with you; you hold me by my right hand' — the pivot from self-condemnation (v. 22) to covenant affirmation. 'Yet I am always with you' — despite the crisis, the confusion, the near-apostasy, the covenant relationship remained intact from YHWH's side. 'You hold me by my right hand' — the guidance image of the stronger leading the weaker: the divine patron grasping the client's hand and preventing the fall that the psalmist described in v. 2. YHWH held him even when the psalmist did not know he was held.
24: 'You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will receive me into glory' — 'you guide me with your counsel' — divine wisdom as the navigation system through the theodicy crisis; the same counsel the psalmist tried to find through his own reflection (v. 16) is available through covenant relationship. 'Afterward you will receive me into glory' (ve'achar kavod tiqacheni) — one of the most discussed verses in the Psalter: the 'receiving into glory' has been read as a this-worldly vindication, as the moment of death taken by YHWH (cf. Ps 49:15), or as anticipating resurrection. The language closely parallels Gen 5:24 (Enoch: 'God took him') and 2 Kgs 2:10 (Elijah's translation).
25: 'Whom in heaven do I have but you? And on earth there is nothing I desire besides you' — the comprehensive declaration of covenant exclusivity: in heaven and on earth, YHWH alone is desired. The double negation covers the entire cosmos: no heavenly being (no member of the divine council, no angel, no idol) and no earthly thing can substitute for YHWH as the object of desire and trust. This is the Shema's implication worked out psychologically: if YHWH is one, then all other objects of desire are relativized. The psalmist has arrived at the affective dimension of monotheism.
26: 'My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever' — 'my flesh and my heart may fail' — the physical-emotional exhaustion of the crisis: the complete depletion of natural resources. But YHWH remains 'the strength of my heart (tsur-levavi, the rock of my heart) and my portion (chelqi) forever.' 'My portion' (cheleq) is the inheritance language of the covenant land allotment: YHWH himself is the psalmist's share, as YHWH was the Levites' portion (Num 18:20: 'I am your portion and your inheritance among the Israelites').
27: 'For behold, those who are far from you will perish; you destroy all who are unfaithful to you' — the complement of v. 25's positive declaration: those who are not in the covenant relationship (far from YHWH) perish. Being 'far' is the opposite of 'being near' in v. 28. 'Unfaithful' (zonim, those who commit prostitution/whoredom) — the covenant-betrayal vocabulary of Hosea and Ezekiel: idolatry as sexual unfaithfulness to the covenant spouse. Those who abandon YHWH for other loyalties are destroyed.
28: 'But as for me, it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord GOD my refuge, that I may declare all your works' — the psalm closes with the psalmist's personal covenant declaration, echoing the wisdom principle of Prov 18:10: 'The name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous person runs to it and is safe.' 'It is good to be near God' — the summary wisdom of the entire journey: after the theodicy crisis, the sanctuary encounter, and the restored perspective, the simplest and most profound conclusion is that proximity to YHWH is the supreme good. The declaration of YHWH's works continues the mission of testimony that characterized Ps 71's aged psalmist.
